response:
{
  "genuinely_different": [
    ["A", "B"],
    ["A", "C"],
    ["B", "C"],
    ["B", "D"],
    ["C", "D"]
  ],
  "superficial_only": [
    ["A", "D"]
  ],
  "same_trajectory_pairs": [],
  "reversal_pairs": [
    ["A", "C", "Model A assumes trust cascade and competitive preemption as key mechanisms; Model C assumes resource bottlenecks (server, personal bandwidth) dominate. If server capacity is insufficient, A's trust cascade could fail while C predicts failure; if server is ample but trust cascade stalls, C might predict success while A predicts failure."],
    ["B", "C", "Model B relies on Product Hunt front-page exposure and procedural viral loops; Model C relies on server capacity and onboarding throughput. If Product Hunt exposure is high but server crashes, B would predict success and C failure; if exposure is low but server is robust and waitlist converts, B predicts failure and C success."]
  ],
  "missing_axes": [
    "Competitor timing and counter-campaign effectiveness (only A mentions competitive preemption, but no model deeply models competitor response dynamics)",
    "Monetization or pricing model impact on user retention (none consider willingness to pay or free-tier conversion)",
    "Seasonality or external events (e.g., New Year resolution effect, back-to-school timing) that could amplify or dampen adoption"
  ],
  "reasoning": "Models A and D share the same core structure: attention cascade via waitlist and Product Hunt, with trust/social proof as the primary mechanism. They differ only in phrasing and minor actor emphasis, making them superficial variants. Models B and C introduce genuinely different decisive constraints: B emphasizes procedural/institutional mechanics (Product Hunt ranking, moderator gatekeeping), while C emphasizes operational resource limits (server capacity, personal bandwidth). These produce different intervention responses and reversal possibilities. Model A's competitive preemption mechanism is unique but not deeply modeled; it is structurally distinct from B and C."
}

prompt:
STRUCTURAL CAUSAL RECONNAISSANCE — you are ONE independent causal-model generator in an
ensemble. You see ONLY this brief; you never see any other generator's output, and no other generator sees
yours. Several materially different causal structures could plausibly determine this outcome — your job is
to construct the strongest COMPLETE causal model visible from your assigned reasoning perspective.

YOUR PERSPECTIVE (adversarial_alternative): the structure the omission critic found missing from every candidate
This perspective shapes what you actively investigate — NOT the scope of the model. Your output must still
be a complete causal model of the question (actors AND institutions AND constraints AND mechanisms), with
your perspective determining which of them you treat as DECISIVE.
